summary:
The Hyksos invasion of Egypt, occurring around 1650 BC, marks one of the most traumatic disruptions in ancient Egyptian history. A people of West Asian origin — the name Hyksos derives from the Egyptian term for "rulers of foreign lands" — the Hyksos had been settling in the Nile Delta for generations before they established direct political control over northern Egypt, founding the Fifteenth Dynasty at the capital of Avaris.

The Hyksos brought significant military and technological innovations to Egypt, including horse-drawn [[event:1859|chariots]], composite bows, bronze weapons, and new agricultural tools such as the vertical loom and improved plows. These technologies gave them a decisive military advantage over the Egyptians, who had no prior experience with chariots or horses as military assets. The shock of Hyksos domination prompted Egyptian rulers of the Seventeenth Dynasty at Thebes to modernize their own military by adopting these same technologies.

The [[event:1800|Second Intermediate Period]] that the Hyksos domination produced was a formative trauma in Egyptian national consciousness. Egyptian texts from subsequent centuries described the Hyksos with deep hostility, portraying their rule as a catastrophic occupation during which temples were desecrated and traditional order subverted. This memory of foreign domination intensified Egyptian xenophobia and provided strong ideological motivation for the subsequent imperial expansion of the [[event:60|New Kingdom]].

Expulsion of the Hyksos was completed by Pharaoh Ahmose I around 1550 BC, who pursued them into the Levant and inaugurated the New Kingdom. The encounter with Hyksos military technology, absorbed and turned against its source, transformed Egypt into a chariot-based military power capable of projecting force far beyond the Nile valley.
